coredata.dc:title: Hybrid nanostructures of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and aromatic amino acids: Synthesis, interactions at interfaces and optical properties
coredata.dc:creator: Trpkov, Djordje
Sredojević, Dušan
Pajović, Jelena
Tošić, Dragana
Božanić, Dušan K.
Djoković, Vladimir
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD) were synthesized using a simple and fast hydrothermal route, employing citric acid and urea as precursors. The resulting NCDs were non-covalently functionalized (conjugated) with aromatic amino acids, namely phenylalanine (Phe) and tryptophan (Trp). Atomic force microscopy revealed that the NCDs exhibit a disk-like morphology with an average diameter of approximately 60 nm and an average height of about 0.5 nm. Following conjugation, the particle height increased to around 3 nm. UV–vis spectroscopy analysis indicated successful conjugation of the amino acids to the NCD nanostructures. Additionally, DFT numerical calculations based on three differently N-doped clusters were performed to elucidate the nature of the non-covalent interactions between NCDs and the corresponding amino acids. Photoluminescent spectra demonstrated a stable and strong fluorescence signal for both hybrids in the UV region. The most significant changes were observed in the case of Trp-conjugation. In contrast to phenylalanine, the non-covalent bonding of tryptophan to NCDs strongly influenced the visible emission (around 500 nm) originating from surface states of the dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tryptophan
Phenylalanine
Carbon dots
DFT
Photoluminescence

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: NCD synthesis and conjugation with aromatic amino acids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM images of CD samples and their corresponding linear height profiles: a) NCDs; b) Trp/NCD; c) Phe/NCD. The inset shows the height profile of a single pristine NCD.

a) FTIR spectra of pristine NCD, phenylalanine amino acid and Phe/NCD hybrid particles; b) FTIR spectra of pristine NCD, tryptophan amino acid and Trp/NCD hybrids Assignation: β, γ- bending and ν - stretching vibrations.
UV–vis spectra of NCD and a) Phe and Phe/NCD hybrid, b) Trp and Trp/NCD hybrid.

PL emission (solid lines) and excitation (dotted lines) spectra of the NCDs and their conjugates with phenylalanine (a and b) and tryptophan (c and d). The experimental conditions i.e. excitation (λexc) and emission (λem) wavelengths are indicated in the graphs.

The optimized structures of a) pristine (P), b) graphitic (G), and c) pyridinic (PY) clusters, as well as the molecular electrostatic potentials (MEPs) on the 0.0004 (electron Bohr−3) electron density isosurfaces of NCD and the two amino acids.
The optimized structures of a) five Phe-complexes and b) five Trp-complexes in the stacking and H-bonding arrangements.

The total density of states (TDOS) of a) Trp/PY and b) Phe/PY complexes (in most stable stacking arrangements) and their constituents, as well as c) the contour plots of molecular orbitals of pristine PY as well as Trp/PY and Phe/PY stacking conformers.
The calculated amino acid-NCD binding energies (ΔE and ΔE CP, kcal∙mol−1) (with and without correction for BSSE (kcal∙mol−1)).

head.ce:title: Hybrid nanostructures of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and aromatic amino acids: Synthesis, interactions at interfaces and optical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences National Institute of the Republic of Serbia, University of Belgrade, P.O. Box 522, Belgrade 11001, Serbia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences National Institute of the Republic of Serbia, University of Belgrade

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences National Institute of the Republic of Serbia, University of Belgrade, PO Box 522, 11001 Belgrade, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Belgrade, Faculty of Physics, P.O. Box 368, Belgrade 11001, Serbia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Belgrade, Faculty of Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Belgrade, Faculty of Physics, P. O. Box 368, 11001 Belgrade, Serbia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD) were synthesized using a simple and fast hydrothermal route, employing citric acid and urea as precursors. The resulting NCDs were non-covalently functionalized (conjugated) with aromatic amino acids, namely phenylalanine (Phe) and tryptophan (Trp). Atomic force microscopy revealed that the NCDs exhibit a disk-like morphology with an average diameter of approximately 60 nm and an average height of about 0.5 nm. Following conjugation, the particle height increased to around 3 nm. UV–vis spectroscopy analysis indicated successful conjugation of the amino acids to the NCD nanostructures. Additionally, DFT numerical calculations based on three differently N-doped clusters were performed to elucidate the nature of the non-covalent interactions between NCDs and the corresponding amino acids. Photoluminescent spectra demonstrated a stable and strong fluorescence signal for both hybrids in the UV region. The most significant changes were observed in the case of Trp-conjugation. In contrast to phenylalanine, the non-covalent bonding of tryptophan to NCDs strongly influenced the visible emission (around 500 nm) originating from surface states of the dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional graphene-like structures with sizes typically ranging from 1 nm to 100 nm [1–5]. They have garnered significant attention due to their remarkable properties, such as high water solubility, tuneable bandgap, stable fluorescence, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [1–8]. In an ideal case, a CD consists of only one carbon atomic layer. However, most synthetic procedures yield less perfect materials with several carbon layers and various oxygen groups (OH, carboxyl, epoxy, etc.). The presence of polar oxygen groups facilitates the easy conjugation of CDs with drugs and active biomolecules, making them an ideal material for applications in drug delivery [8]. Additionally, the conjugation of CDs with biomolecules has proven to be a successful strategy for bioimaging and biosensing applications [9–11].

A recent review by Pichumani and co-workers [12] suggested that functionalizing carbonaceous quantum dots with amino acids could be an effective route to enhance their electronic and optical properties. It was further shown that the conjugation of tryptophan to graphene quantum dots increased the capacity of the nanostructure to adsorb the hydrophobic anti-cancer agent curcumin and enabled its pH-sensitive release [13]. In line with this research, our present study investigates hybrid materials of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and aromatic amino acids (tryptophan and phenylalanine). The focus of the study lies in the optical properties of these systems, with an emphasis on the potential modes of interaction between NCD particles and amino acid molecules. A better understanding of the effects at the interfaces is important considering that the hybrids of carbon dots and fluorescent molecules have already found applications in fluorescent bioimaging [14] or as fluorescent sensors [15]. Additionally, the interaction-induced changes in the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots were successfully used for the selective detection of certain biomolecules, including L-tryptophan [16].

Nitrogen doping strongly influences the electronic properties and photoluminescence of CDs. By altering reaction parameters (solvent, temperature, time) and source materials, it is possible to obtain NCDs that emit in the blue, green, or yellow part of the electromagnetic spectrum [17,18]. It has been suggested that, during hydrothermal synthesis, nitrogen atoms are incorporated into CDs through intramolecular dehydroxylation reactions between neighbouring carboxyl and amide groups [17]. The formed pyrrolic N-structures are further transformed into graphite N-structures as the reaction progresses [17]. The quantum yield of NCDs was found to be dependent on the type of amines (primary, secondary, and ternary) used as precursors, and consequently, on the level of doping. Due to their excellent optical properties, NCDs have been successfully utilized in bioimaging [18], photovoltaic [19], energy storage [20] and photocatalytic applications [21]. It has also been reported that the combination of nitrogen doping and functionalization with amino groups can produce highly efficient dots for two-photon bioimaging [22].

In our previous report [23], blue luminescent N-doped CDs were hydrothermally synthesized in glycerol using glycine as a precursor. In the present study, we employed a slightly different procedure with urea as a source of N-atoms, resulting in the formation of green and yellow emission NCDs. These NCDs were then conjugated with luminescent amino acids (tryptophan and phenylalanine), and the hybrid particles obtained were characterized using microscopic and spectroscopic methods to reveal their morphology and optical properties. Considering that N atoms provide active sites for conjugation with biomolecules, density functional theory calculations based on three differently N-doped clusters (ideal and with mono-atomic vacancies) were performed to elucidate the nature of interactions between NCDs and the corresponding amino acids.

Citric acid (CA) and phenylalanine (Phe) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Tryptophan and urea were obtained from J&K scientific Ltd. and Kemika, Zagreb, respectively. Ultrapure water was used in all experimental procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NCD synthesis and conjugation with aromatic amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: NCD were using the hydrothermal method with citric acid and urea as precursors. In a typical procedure, a mixture of CA and urea in a molar ratio of 1:3 was dissolved in 12.5 ml of deionized water and left in the oven at 180 °C for 4 hours. The resulting dark syrup was dispersed in deionized water. The stock solution of NCDs was obtained as the supernatant after centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 minutes. The concentration of NCD solution (c = 8.5 mg/ml) was determined from the mass of the remaining powder after drying. Amino acid/NCD hybrids were prepared by mixing 5 ml of 0.04 M amino acid and 5 ml of 0.01 M NCD aqueous solutions with continuous stirring for 1 h. The preparation of the samples is schematically presented in Figure S1 (Supporting information).

FT-IR spectra were recorded in the region from 400 to 4000 cm−1 (with a resolution of 0.5 cm−1) using a Nicolet iS50 FT-IR Thermo Fisher Scientific spectrophotometer equipped with Smart iTR attenuated total reflectance (ATR) sampling accessory. The sample powders were placed on a diamond plate and fixed with a pressure tower.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were conducted using a Quesant atomic force microscope. 20 µL of NCD dispersion was deposited on a freshly cleaved mica surface using a spin coating method (3500 rpm, 1 min). Images were recorded in tapping mode at room temperature using standard silicon probes Q-WM300 with force constant 40 Nm−1 (Nano and More, Germany).

Photoluminescent (PL) properties of materials were characterized by Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4 spectrometer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan). The excitation wavelengths ranged from 200 to 500 nm. The samples were diluted in ultrapure water and the spectra were collected in ambient conditions in the quartz cuvette.
The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Thermo Evolution 600 spectrophotometer.

Three N-doped graphene-like clusters were constructed as model systems for DFT calculations to study their adsorption properties with amino acids (AA: Phe & Trp) and the electronic structures of the corresponding adducts. These clusters include graphene-like cores with pristine, graphitic, and pyridinic N-doped CDs morphologies, incorporating amino, carboxyl, and amide groups at their edges. Ground-state geometry optimizations of separated NCDs and AA, as well as AA/NCD complexes, were conducted in the gas phase using the ωB97xD hybrid functional [24] in combination with the Pople's valence double-ζ polarized 6–31+G(d,p) basis set [25] implemented in Gaussian 09 program [26]. Harmonic frequency analyses were performed at the same level of theory to prove that optimized structures present minima with no imaginary frequencies. Excited state calculations were performed using the TD-DFT at the same level of theory. The counterpoise method was employed to determine the intermolecular interaction energies (ΔE CP) with corrections for basis set superposition errors (BSSE) [27], following the equations:
(1) ΔE = E Complex – (E NCD + E AA)
(2) ΔE CP = ΔE + E BSSE

Negative energy values indicate energetically favourable adsorption processes. The GaussView software [28] was used to obtain the optimized geometry of the clusters, MEP maps, and molecular orbitals. Simultaneously, the GaussSum program [29] was employed to generate DOS diagrams and IR spectra.

The morphology of carbon dots was investigated using force microscopy. As shown in Fig. 1, AFM images reveal that carbon dots, both before and after functionalization, exhibit a circular shape with a uniform size distribution. The AFM analysis suggested that the average diameter of the particles was found to be ∼60 nm. This is also confirmed with TEM measurements. The TEM image (Supporting information, Figure S2) clearly shows the particles with diameters in the range from 40 to 70 nm.

The AFM analysis also revealed an increase in the average height of nanoparticles after functionalization with amino acids. Based on the measurements of individual profiles of the nanoparticles, it was estimated that the average height of pristine NCDs is 0.5 nm (Fig. 1a), while the heights of the Trp/NCD (Fig. 1b) and Phe/NCD (Fig. 1c) hybrids were 3 nm and 2.5 nm, respectively. The inset in Fig. 1a illustrates the characteristic height profile of a single NCD with a height of 0.35 nm. More detailed representations of height profiles for specific carbon dots in all samples are included in the Supplementary material (Figure S3).

FTIR spectra of NCDs, pure amino acids (tryptophan and phenylalanine) and their corresponding hybrids are presented in Fig. 2. In the spectrum of pure NCDs, typical vibrations originating from N-containing functional groups can be observed. Primary amines typically exhibit a double band in the 3300–3500 cm−1 range, and the lines at 3421 and 3330 cm−1 are attributed to N-H stretching vibrations [30]. The line at 1583 cm−1 corresponds to N-H bending vibration of primary amines, while the line at 1155 cm−1 originates from stretching vibrations of C-N group (Fig. 2a). The band at 1462 cm−1, corresponding to aromatic C-C stretching vibrations, confirms that the dots have a graphene-like structure [30].

In Fig. 2a, it is observed that after conjugation with phenylalanine, the N-H stretching bands shift to 3448 and 3413 cm−1, respectively. The intensities of the FTIR spectral lines in the Phe/NCD hybrid are reduced compared to the spectral lines of pure phenylalanine. Phenylalanine exhibts typical amino acid bands including stretching vibration of COO- at 1555 cm−1, bending vibration of amine at 1493 cm−1, while the bands in 600–900 cm−1 range originate from out of plane C-H bending vibrations in the aromatic ring. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 2b, the spectral features of the pure tryptophan and Trp/NCD hybrid are virtually identical. This suggests that tryptophan is strongly bound to the NCD surfaces, hindering the signal coming from the carbon dot vibrational modes.

Fig. 3 shows UV vis spectra of NCDs, pure amino acids (tryptophan and phenylalanine) and their corresponding conjugates. It can be seen that both NCDs and the pure amino acids absorb in the UV part of the EM spectrum. NCDs particles exhibit an additional broad absorbing feature at ∼410 nm that originates from n-π* surface state transitions of the attached amino functional groups [31]. In the case of phenylalanine (Fig. 3a), the absorption spectrum of the hybrid particles closely resembles that of the spectrum of NCDs. Typical absorption peaks at 240 nm and 275 nm, attributed to sp2 carbons and carbonyl groups from graphene, also appear in the spectrum of the Phe/NCD hybrids [31]. Trp/NCD hybrids exhibit somewhat different behaviour. As shown in Fig. 3b, the π-π* transition from sp2 carbons in graphene benzene rings is not present in the spectrum of NCD-Trp hybrid particles. Moreover, tryptophan exhibits a strong absorption at 280 nm, arising from the π-π* transitions of the indole group [32], and this feature is preserved after the conjugation to NCDs.

Fig. 4 illustrates photoluminescence (PL) and excitation spectra of NCDs and their conjugates with tryptophan and phenylalanine. PL spectra were obtained with UV (in range 210–280 nm) and visible (410 nm) excitation wavelengths. After excitation at 210 nm, the PL spectrum of the pure the NCDs exhibits two emission features: a shoulder peak at 305 nm (∼4.1 eV) and a strong intensity peak at 355 nm (3.5 eV) originating from the intrinsic C and graphitic N carbon-core levels [33], respectively (Fig. 4a). Excitation of as-prepared NCDs at 410 nm also produces a broad emission at 525 nm, arising from the surface state levels of the CO group [33] (Fig. 4b). In Figs. 4a and 4b, the PL spectra of the Phe/NCD hybrids, obtained at excitation wavelengths of 210 or 410 nm, exhibit emission features originating from their individual components. The emission peak of Phe at ∼280 nm overlaps the emission from intrinsic C-levels of the dots (Fig. 4a), while the emission from surface states remains virtually unaffected after the adsorption of the amino acid (Fig. 4b).

In Fig. 4c, the primary fluorescence emission of tryptophan amino acid closely aligns with the emission of NCD particles in the region from 325 to 425 nm. Consequently, the emission spectrum of the Trp/NCD particles, obtained with UV excitation (270 nm), could be described as a superposition of NCD and Trp peaks. The main disparity in the fluorescence behavior of the Trp/NCD and Phe/NCD conjugates is evident in the case of excitation at 410 nm (Fig. 4d). Conjugation with tryptophan noticeably diminishes the emission of NCD surface states. On the other hand, the excitation spectra of NCD and Trp/NCD obtained at λem = 520 nm (dotted lines in Fig. 4d) suggest that the reduced emission observed for Trp/NCD hybrid is not a consequence of radiation quenching. The intensity of the excitation peak of the NCD hybrid at ∼410 nm also decreases after the conjugation of dots with tryptophan. Our DFT calculations of the amino acid-carbon dot cluster interactions, presented in detail in the next section, suggest that this reduction is more likely a consequence of the changes in the surface state levels due to orbital mixing. A comparison of calculated density of states of N-doped carbon dot cluster before and after interaction with tryptophan (Fig. 7) implies that conjugation induces changes in HOMO and LUMO energy states. Therfore, the absorption of the 410 nm light is reduced, which can be clearly seen in the excitation spectrum of Trp/NCD hybrid.

To investigate the adsorption properties of N-doped CDs toward the aromatic amino acids (Phe & Trp), we utilized DFT calculations. Ab initio techniques offer an atomistic representation of differently doped and functionalized CDs explaining their electronic and optical features. We employed these methods to calculate the density of states diagrams (DOS), frontier molecular orbitals (FMO), and molecular electrostatic potentials (MEPs) of the hybrid structures and their individual components. These calculations provide information about the most stable geometries of the components (NCDs or amino acids) as well as their binding preferences.

As described in the literature [34], CD with different N-doping types can exhibit diverse behaviours. We constructed three different NCDs in the form of graphene-like clusters designated as pristine (P), graphitic (G), and pyridinic (PY) ( Fig. 5). All three clusters possess the same side groups at the same positions: two amino and one carboxyl and amide groups. The pristine (P) cluster consists of 19 fused C6-rings in an ideal honeycomb structure. The graphitic (G) structure has four embedded N-atoms into the graphene core, whereas pyridinic (PY) has four nitrogen atoms instilled into the defective structure with two-point vacancy sites. The pristine (P) cluster remains planar after the optimization, while the graphitic and pyridinic structures become slightly distorted.

Molecular electrostatic potentials (MEPs) were analysed to describe the binding sites of relevant species (Fig. 5). The MEP values are represented as follows: green denotes areas with zero potential, while red and blue indicate the electron-rich (negative) and electron-deficient (positive) parts, respectively. Negative (red) regions are consistently located around the -COOH and -CONH2 groups, while electropositive (blue) regions are found at the edges of the clusters. The central rings of P and PY clusters are electron-rich with high electron density surrounding the vacancy site of the PY-cluster. In contrast, central rings in the G-structure are electron-deficient, especially around the N-atoms (Fig. 5). Regarding the amino acids, the aromatic rings of Trp are more negatively charged than that of Phe.

Calculating binding energies is crucial for predicting the strength of intermolecular non-covalent interactions in AA/NCD systems. The optimized structures of five Phe/cluster complexes are depicted in Fig. 6a, while the binding energies (ΔE & ΔE CP) are summarized in Table 1. Initially, we selected the most stable conformer for stacking interactions between Phe and the P-cluster; the other two are provided in the ESI (Figure S4). Subsequently, we used this conformer for further estimation of stacking interactions involving G and PY clusters. The strongest stacking interaction was calculated for the Phe/G system (ΔE CP = −21.76 kcal mol−1), while the weakest was found in the Phe/PY system (ΔE CP = −17.40 kcal mol−1). The incorporation of the N-atoms into the graphene core evidently renders the central rings more positive (Fig. 5; MEPs). This effect induces stronger electrostatic interactions and, consequently, the most robust overall binding between Phe and the G-cluster. On the other hand, the absence of two atoms in the PY-cluster leads to the weakest binding due to reduced dispersion interactions and the accumulation of negative charges around the vacancy site (Fig. 6; MEPs).

Hydrogen bonding energies are computed, taking into account side carboxyl and amide groups (Fig. 6a). The predicted H-bond distances, ranging from 1.62 to 1.83 Å, classify them as strong hydrogen bonding according to Jeffrey et al. (< 2.5 Å; strong, primarily covalent) [35]. It was observed that the Phe makes somewhat stronger hydrogen bonds with the carboxyl group than with the amide group of the P-cluster (-22.31 vs. −20.54 kcal/mol). Based on the data from Table 1, it can be concluded that H-bonding interactions are slightly more favourable than stacking interactions in Phe-complexes.

For the Trp/NCD systems, we also computed the interaction energies in stacking and H-bonding arrangements (Fig. 6b). Similar to Phe, the strongest stacking of tryptophan was estimated for the G cluster (ΔE CP = −25.71 kcal mol−1) and the weakest stacking for the PY cluster (ΔE CP = −21.60 kcal mol−1). On the other hand, compared to Phe-complexes, the stacking interactions in Trp-complexes are a few kcal mol−1 stronger (Table 1).

This implies that the dispersion forces between clusters and tryptophan’s indole ring are higher than those with the phenyl ring of Phe. Additionally, the indole ring is more negatively charged than phenyl (Fig. 6; MEPs), resulting in stronger electrostatic interactions with clusters. In contrast, the strengths of hydrogen bonds through which two amino acids interact with the pristine cluster (P) are very similar. As noticed in Table 1, the corresponding carboxyl and amide hydrogen bond energies estimated for Phe/P and Trp/P complexes are virtually equal. Nonetheless, given that all these energies are comparable, one could expect both types of interactions (stacking and H-bonding) may be formed between aromatic AA and NCDs in the aqueous solution.
The total densities of states (TDOS) of the Trp/PY and Phe/PY complexes (in the most stable stacking arrangement) as well as their components are shown in Fig. 7.

The DOS diagrams reveal that the HOMO orbitals of Trp and PY-cluster are close in energy. Consequently, in their most stable stacking adduct (Trp/PY), Trp’s HOMO is inserted inside the PY bandgap region. The binding of Trp also induces perturbation in electron density and molecular orbitals order in the PY-cluster (Fig. 7c). This phenomenon might be responsible for the reduced visible emission observed in the Trp/NCD system (Fig. 4d). On the other hand, the HOMO of Phe is significantly below the PY-cluster’s HOMO, and, as a result, in the stacking conformation, there is no electron perturbation in FMO levels of PY (Fig. 7c). This observation aligns with the fact that the emission of NCD surface states at 520 nm is not affected by conjugation with phenylalanine (Fig. 4b).

Functionalization of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD) with aromatic amino acids (phenylalanine (Phe), tryptophan (Trp)) produces hybrid nanostructures suitable for various applications. The properties of these nanostructures strongly depend on the nature of the amino acid-NCD interactions, prompting this study to elucidate potential processes at the interfaces. Given that NCD contains core (graphitic N) and surface (amino and oxygen) polar groups various possible modes of interaction arise. DFT calculations suggested that both stacking and strong H-bonding contribute to the conjugation of amino acid to the NCD. Although the amino acids are non-covalently attached onto the NCDs, the optical properties of the Phe/NCD and Trp/NCD hybrids differ. UV–vis spectroscopy reveals that typical absorption peaks of sp2 carbons and carbonyl groups of NCD are present in the spectrum of Phe/NCD but they are absent in the spectrum of Trp/NCD. The fluorescence properties of the components of the hybrid structures were preserved after the conjugation. Both hybrids exhibit strong fluorescence in the UV part of the electromagnetic spectrum. However, conjugation with tryptophan reduces the visible emissions from the surface states of NCD. In general, the conclusion is that even a slight change in the size of the amino acid aromatic group (e.g. from phenyl to indole) can affect the strength of the interaction with NCDs and, consequently, the final properties of the obtained hybrid nanostructures.

Vladimir Djokovic: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Djordje Trpkov: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Dragana Tošić: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Dušan K. Božanić: Visualization, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Dušan Sredojević: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jelena Pajović: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was funded by the Ministry of Science, Technological development and Innovation of the Republic of Serbia (Grants: 451–03–66/2024–03/200017 and 451–03–66/2024–03/200162).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.113878.
